questions.question:
On a planar field, you travelled 3 units East from a point O. Next you travelled 4 units South to arrive at point P. Then you travelled from P in the North-East direction such that you arrive at a point that is 6 units East of point O. Next, you travelled in the North-West direction, so that you arrive at point Q. The distance of point Q to point O (in the same units) should be

A. 3
B. 4
C. 5
D. 6

understand.plain: You move on a 2-D plane, first East then South to reach P, then diagonally North-East until directly east of O by 6 units, and finally North-West to reach Q. The question asks for the straight-line distance OQ. By setting up coordinates and using the Pythagorean theorem the distance works out to exactly 5 units.

keywords.explain: Moving at 45 degrees between North and East means equal x and y increments with each step.
keywords.example: From (3,-4) moving NE: x and y both increase by the same amount per unit distance.

keywords.explain: Moving at 45 degrees between North and West means x decreases and y increases by equal amounts per unit distance.
keywords.example: From (6,-1) moving NW: each unit of travel decreases x by 1/sqrt(2) and increases y by 1/sqrt(2).

keywords.explain: Straight-line distance between two points (x1,y1) and (x2,y2) is sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2).

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 280' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='280' rx='10' class='bg'/><g transform='translate(40,230)'><line x1='0' y1='0' x2='240' y2='0' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='0' y1='0' x2='0' y2='-200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><text x='245' y='5' class='sub mut'>E</text><text x='-10' y='-205' class='sub mut'>N</text><circle cx='0' cy='0' r='5' class='ok'/><text x='-18' y='12' class='tag ink'>O</text><circle cx='60' cy='0' r='5' fill='#7F77DD'/><circle cx='60' cy='-80' r='5' class='no'/><text x='65' y='-75' class='tag ink'>P</text><circle cx='120' cy='-60' r='5' fill='#7F77DD'/><circle cx='0' cy='-120' r='5' class='ok'/><text x='-18' y='-115' class='tag ink'>Q</text><line x1='0' y1='0' x2='60' y2='0' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='25' y='-6' class='sub ok'>3E</text><line x1='60' y1='0' x2='60' y2='-80' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='65' y='-35' class='sub no'>4S</text><line x1='60' y1='-80' x2='120' y2='-60' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='80' y='-85' class='sub ink'>NE</text><line x1='120' y1='-60' x2='0' y2='-120' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='55' y='-105' class='sub ink'>NW</text><line x1='0' y1='0' x2='0' y2='-120' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='5,4'/><text x='5' y='-60' class='sub mut'>?</text><text x='6' y='-55' class='sub mut'>5</text></g></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A coordinate diagram showing O at origin, 3 steps East, 4 steps South to P, then NE to a point 6 East of O, then NW to Q directly above O at distance 5.

given.aim: Find the straight-line distance OQ after a four-leg journey on a plane.

terms.example: All distances are measured from O.
terms.connects: reference point for the final distance calculation

terms.meaning: Intermediate point reached after moving 3 East and 4 South from O; coordinates (3, -4).
terms.connects: starting point of the NE leg

terms.meaning: Final destination after the NW leg; its distance from O is what we need.
terms.example: Q turns out to be at (0, 5) giving OQ = 5.
terms.connects: endpoint whose distance from O is the answer

terms.meaning: For a right triangle with legs a and b the hypotenuse c satisfies c^2 = a^2 + b^2.
terms.connects: used to compute distance OQ once coordinates of Q are known

given.plan: Assign coordinates to every waypoint using the cardinal directions and 45-degree diagonal rules. Then express Q as a point on the NW ray from the intermediate point and solve for its position, using the Pythagorean theorem for the final distance.

steps.title: Assign coordinates to each waypoint
steps.apply: O = (0, 0). After 3 East: (3, 0). After 4 South: P = (3, -4). NE from P means equal x and y increments. To reach x = 6 (6 units East of O): delta_x = 3, so delta_y = 3 (NE is 45 deg). Intermediate point R = (3+3, -4+3) = (6, -1).
steps.note: NE direction means slope +1, so x and y change equally.

steps.title: Find Q on the NW ray from R and compute OQ
steps.apply: NW from R=(6,-1): subtract t from x and add t to y, giving Q = (6-t, -1+t). Distance OQ = sqrt((6-t)^2 + (t-1)^2) = sqrt(2t^2 - 14t + 37). Setting this equal to 5: 2t^2 - 14t + 12 = 0, so t^2 - 7t + 6 = 0, giving t = 1 (Q = (5,0)) or t = 6 (Q = (0,5)). Both give OQ = 5.
steps.note: Both solutions are geometrically valid; the answer is uniquely 5 regardless of which intersection is chosen.

questions.formula_note: The Pythagorean distance formula converts the coordinate differences into the required straight-line distance.

questions.question:
An ant walks in a straight line on a plane leaving behind a trace of its movement. The initial position of the ant is at point P facing east. The ant first turns 72 degrees anticlockwise at P, and then does the following two steps in sequence exactly FIVE times before halting.

1. moves forward for 10 cms
2. turns 144 degrees clockwise

The pattern made by the trace left behind by the ant is

A. [regular pentagon]
B. [irregular star]
C. [regular 5-pointed star / pentagram]
D. [open spiral]

understand.plain: The ant starts at P facing East, makes one initial 72-degree left turn, and then repeats the same move-and-turn pair exactly five times. Each leg is 10 cm long and each turn is 144 degrees clockwise. Because 5 x 144 = 720 degrees (two full turns), the ant ends exactly back at P, and the five line segments trace out a regular pentagram (5-pointed star). The initial 72-degree turn ensures the first segment heads in the correct direction to close the star symmetrically.

keywords.explain: A five-pointed star formed by connecting every second vertex of a regular pentagon; each internal turn equals 144 degrees.
keywords.example: Drawing a star by skipping one vertex each time on a circle with 5 equally spaced points.

keywords.explain: When tracing a pentagram, each clockwise turn is 144 degrees, which is the exterior angle; 5 x 144 = 720 = 2 x 360, so the pencil turns exactly twice to close the figure.
keywords.example: Compare with a regular pentagon where each exterior turn is 72 degrees and 5 x 72 = 360.

keywords.explain: Anticlockwise is a left turn (positive angle convention); clockwise is a right turn. The initial 72-degree anticlockwise turn sets the correct starting heading for the star.
keywords.example: Facing East and turning 72 degrees anticlockwise means the ant now faces 72 degrees above the East axis (North-Northeast direction).

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='300' height='300' rx='10' class='bg'/><g transform='translate(150,150)'><polygon points='0,-95 90,69 -73,-28 73,-28 -90,69' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3' stroke-linejoin='round'/><circle cx='0' cy='-95' r='5' class='ok'/><text x='5' y='-98' class='tag ink'>P</text><circle cx='90' cy='69' r='4' class='no'/><circle cx='-73' cy='-28' r='4' class='no'/><circle cx='73' cy='-28' r='4' class='no'/><circle cx='-90' cy='69' r='4' class='no'/><text x='-50' y='15' class='sub mut'>144 deg</text><text x='-50' y='30' class='sub mut'>clockwise</text><text x='-50' y='45' class='sub mut'>each turn</text></g></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A regular five-pointed star (pentagram) with vertices labeled, showing the five equal legs of 10 cm each connected by 144-degree clockwise turns.

given.aim: Determine the geometric shape traced by the ant's path after the described sequence of moves and turns.

terms.meaning: The ant faces East (0 deg) and turns 72 deg anticlockwise, so it now heads at 72 deg from East (nearly NNE).
terms.example: Heading 72 deg means direction vector (cos72, sin72) = (0.309, 0.951).
terms.connects: determines the direction of the first 10-cm leg

terms.meaning: Each 144-degree clockwise turn is the exterior angle of the pentagram; after 5 such turns the total rotation is 720 = 2 x 360 deg, closing the path.
terms.example: A regular pentagon uses 72-deg exterior angles; a pentagram uses 144-deg angles (skipping one vertex).
terms.connects: explains why the ant returns exactly to P and why the figure is a star

terms.meaning: A path with equal-length legs and equal turning angles closes when the total turning angle is a multiple of 360 degrees.
terms.example: 5 x 144 = 720 = 2 x 360, so the ant closes after exactly 5 legs.
terms.connects: confirms the traced shape is a closed regular pentagram

terms.meaning: The five vertices of the pentagram lie on a circle equally spaced at 72 deg apart, but each leg connects every second vertex (skipping one), creating the star shape.
terms.example: Vertices at 90, 162, 234, 306, 378 (=18) deg on a circle produce the pentagram.
terms.connects: geometric description of the pattern produced

given.plan: Track the ant's heading after each turn and verify that all five legs are equal-length and the path closes. Identify the pattern by noting the 144-degree turns match the exterior angle of a pentagram.

steps.title: Compute the heading after the initial turn
steps.apply: Ant starts facing East = 0 deg. Turns 72 deg anticlockwise: new heading = 72 deg. This is the direction of the first leg.
steps.note: Anticlockwise turns increase the angle in standard convention.

steps.title: Trace all five legs and verify path closure
steps.apply: Leg 1: heading 72 deg, end at (10*cos72, 10*sin72) = (3.09, 9.51). Turn -144 -> heading -72 deg. Leg 2: end at (6.18, 0.00). Turn -144 -> heading -216 deg = 144 deg. Leg 3: end at (-1.91, 5.88). Turn -144 -> heading 0 deg. Leg 4: end at (8.09, 5.88). Turn -144 -> heading -144 deg. Leg 5: end at (0.00, 0.00). The ant returns exactly to P.
steps.note: All five legs are 10 cm; the path is closed and forms a regular pentagram.

steps.title: Identify the shape
steps.apply: Five equal legs with 144-degree clockwise turns between them produce a regular five-pointed star (pentagram). The initial 72-degree CCW turn orients the star so its topmost vertex is directly above P.
steps.note: A regular pentagon would use 72-degree turns; 144-degree turns skip every other vertex, creating the star.

solution.result: The ant traces a regular five-pointed star (pentagram). Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: No standard formula from the registry applies; the solution uses angle arithmetic and vector addition to trace the path.

formulas.name: Pythagorean Distance Between Two Points
formulas.plain: The straight-line distance between points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) is sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2), derived directly from the Pythagorean theorem on the right triangle formed by the coordinate differences.
